title: Accountants and auditors
exposure_rationale: Accountancy is a fundamentally digital occupation centered on data analysis, regulatory compliance, and reporting, all of which are highly susceptible to AI and robotic process automation. While high-level advisory roles and complex forensic audits require human judgment and relationship management, the core tasks of data organization, tax computation, and anomaly detection are increasingly being automated, significantly increasing worker productivity and restructuring the entry-level labor market.
tier_rationale: An AI agent for accountants/auditors requires deep integration with each client's general ledger, ERP (NetSuite, SAP, QuickBooks), tax filing systems, and document management, plus configuration to the company's chart of accounts, policies, and regulatory regime. The skills are standardized (GAAP, tax code) but useful deployment demands per-customer data plumbing, controls, and audit-trail compliance — matching the Harvey/Abridge pattern. Forensic and advisory work has tacit elements, but the bulk of the occupation fits T2.

title: Actuaries
exposure_rationale: Actuarial work is fundamentally digital, involving the analysis of large datasets, statistical modeling, and financial forecasting—all areas where AI and machine learning excel. While the profession requires high-level human judgment and the ability to explain complex risks to stakeholders, the core technical tasks of data compilation and probability estimation are highly susceptible to automation and significant productivity gains through AI.
tier_rationale: Actuarial work requires deep integration with company-specific data (policy databases, claims history, mortality tables, pension records) and tuning to each insurer's product lines, regulatory jurisdiction, and reserving methodologies. While the mathematical and statistical skills are highly standardized, an AI agent serving an actuarial function would need substantial per-customer configuration to access internal data warehouses, replicate company-specific models, and integrate with regulatory reporting workflows. The agent product is genericizable across the industry but each enterprise deployment requires significant integration work.

title: Adult basic and secondary education and ESL teachers
exposure_rationale: The core work product—language instruction, literacy training, and test preparation—is fundamentally digital and information-based, making it highly susceptible to AI-driven tutoring and translation tools. While the role requires significant cultural sensitivity and interpersonal motivation, AI can now provide personalized lesson plans, real-time feedback, and adaptive learning paths that significantly increase teacher productivity or replace routine instructional tasks.
tier_rationale: Teaching adult basic education and ESL involves standard curricula (high school equivalency subjects, English language fundamentals) and uses common tools (Canvas, standard lesson materials). A horizontal AI tutor agent could deliver lessons, assess progress, and adapt instruction across any program without per-customer integration. The in-person classroom and relational components are real but the core knowledge work — lesson planning, content delivery, assessment — is highly genericizable.

title: Advertising sales agents
exposure_rationale: The core of this occupation involves digital knowledge work such as analyzing sales statistics, preparing media kits, and drafting contracts, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation. While the role requires interpersonal relationship building, the shift toward programmatic advertising and AI-driven ad placement is already reducing the need for human intermediaries in the sales process, as reflected in the projected employment decline.
tier_rationale: Advertising sales is fundamentally relational: success depends on cultivating trust with specific clients, repeated cold-call persistence, and rapport-based negotiation. An AI agent could automate prospect research, proposal drafting, media kit prep, and pipeline tracking, but the core sales relationship and persuasion calls remain with the human. This makes it a T3c relational role rather than a genericizable or framework-customizable one.

title: Advertising, promotions, and marketing managers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is predominantly digital and information-based, involving market research, data analysis, and content strategy—all areas where AI is rapidly advancing. While high-level strategic decision-making and stakeholder management provide some insulation, AI can now automate significant portions of campaign planning, creative drafting, and consumer data processing, leading to major restructuring of the role.
tier_rationale: Marketing managers' work combines strategic decision-making with relational coordination across internal teams (sales, finance, executives), external clients, agencies, and media partners. While agents can augment portable tasks like market research, campaign analysis, copy drafting, and media planning, the core managerial work involves negotiating contracts, persuading stakeholders, and maintaining client/agency relationships that stay with the human. There's also a tacit judgment component (brand instinct, creative direction) but the relational dimension dominates at this management level.

title: Aerospace engineers
exposure_rationale: Aerospace engineering is a high-level knowledge occupation where core tasks like aerodynamic modeling, structural analysis, and propulsion design are increasingly performed using digital simulation and AI-driven optimization tools. While the role requires critical human judgment for safety, regulatory compliance, and physical inspection of prototypes, the heavy reliance on computer-aided design (CAD) and complex data analysis makes it highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and task automation.
tier_rationale: Aerospace engineering involves deep tacit judgment about safety-critical systems (structural margins, aerodynamic intuition, failure modes) developed over years on specific airframes and programs, combined with proprietary CAD/CAE toolchains, classified defense data, and program-specific design heritage that can't be cleanly extracted. An agent useful here would need to be harness-engineered by the senior engineers themselves, iterating on their own simulation and design workflows. While some sub-tasks (documentation, basic analysis) could be genericized, the core design judgment resists articulation and standardization.

title: Air traffic controllers
exposure_rationale: The core work is fundamentally digital and data-driven, involving the monitoring of radar screens and the processing of complex spatial information to ensure aircraft separation. While the high-stakes nature of safety and the need for real-time human communication with pilots provide a buffer, AI and automated systems (like NextGen) are already increasing productivity and handling more traffic per controller, leading to a very low projected growth in human employment.
tier_rationale: Air traffic control is knowledge work (decision-making, communication, monitoring), but an agent would require deep integration with FAA-specific radar systems, NextGen infrastructure, airspace configurations, and facility-specific procedures. The skills are standardized across the industry but deployment requires substantial per-facility configuration and certification against safety-critical systems. Note: actual deployment is also constrained by regulation, but the shape is T2.

title: Arbitrators, mediators, and conciliators
exposure_rationale: This occupation is primarily digital and information-based, involving the analysis of legal documents, evidence, and the drafting of settlement agreements—tasks where AI is rapidly achieving high proficiency. While the role requires significant interpersonal skills and emotional intelligence to manage confrontational parties, the core analytical and administrative functions are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and partial automation.
tier_rationale: The core work is facilitating dialogue between disputing parties, requiring trust, neutrality, and in-person relational presence with emotionally charged humans. While an agent could assist with document review, precedent research, and drafting settlement agreements, the relational core—building rapport, reading the room, and being accepted as a neutral third party—must remain with the human. Parties consent to a specific mediator/arbitrator, making this fundamentally relational.

title: Architects
exposure_rationale: Architects perform high-level knowledge work that is increasingly digital, utilizing CADD and BIM software which is highly susceptible to AI-driven automation in generative design and technical documentation. While the role requires physical site visits and complex client negotiations, a significant portion of the core work—drafting, specification writing, and regulatory compliance—is moving toward high AI integration.
tier_rationale: Architecture combines client relationships, creative judgment, and site-specific tacit knowledge with standard tools (CADD/BIM). While drafting and code-checking can be done by horizontal agents on standard files, the core work involves sustained client relationships to elicit requirements, negotiate design tradeoffs, and manage contractor relationships through construction — value that lives in specific relational trust. The portable parts (drawings, specs, code research) are augmentable T1-style, but the role's core is relational.

title: Architectural and engineering managers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is predominantly knowledge-based and digital, involving project planning, budgeting, and technical oversight that can be significantly enhanced by AI tools. While the role requires high-level human judgment, leadership, and physical site visits, the core tasks of analyzing technical data, scheduling, and coordinating complex engineering workflows are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation.
tier_rationale: A&E managers combine technical judgment (evaluating designs, anticipating failure modes) with relational leadership (hiring, motivating teams, stakeholder coordination) and deep company/project-specific tacit context. Much of the value comes from engineering gestalt that even the manager can't fully articulate, so only the expert themselves could harness an agent to replicate it. Relational and T3a elements are present, but the genuinely tacit technical judgment dominates.

title: Art directors
exposure_rationale: Art directors work in a fundamentally digital domain where AI is rapidly advancing in image generation, layout design, and video synthesis. While the role requires high-level human judgment, leadership, and client communication, the core work product can be heavily augmented or automated by AI, significantly increasing productivity and potentially reducing the number of directors needed to oversee creative projects.
tier_rationale: Art directors' work centers on translating client vision into creative direction and leading teams of artists — value lives in client relationships, taste negotiation, and stakeholder management. An AI agent could augment portable parts (mood boards, layout drafts, reviewing artwork) but the relational core of pitching to clients and directing creative staff stays with the human. Aspects are also tacit (creative judgment), but the relational dimension dominates.

title: Atmospheric scientists, including meteorologists
exposure_rationale: The core of this occupation involves analyzing digital data, running computer models, and writing code, all of which are highly susceptible to AI advancement. Machine learning is already revolutionizing weather forecasting by outperforming traditional numerical models in speed and efficiency, potentially reducing the number of human forecasters needed for routine data processing and report generation.
tier_rationale: Atmospheric scientists work with standard meteorological data sources (NOAA models, radar, satellites) but operational deployment requires integration with specific data feeds, forecasting systems, and client-specific needs (airline routing, utility load forecasting, broadcast graphics pipelines). An AI agent could be built as a product but each deployment requires connecting to the customer's data infrastructure and tuning outputs to their decision workflows. Research meteorology tasks lean more horizontal, but the operational forecasting majority fits T2.

title: Bill and account collectors
exposure_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, consisting of data analysis, skip tracing, and communication via phone or computer. AI and automated systems are already driving a projected 10% decline in employment by handling routine notifications and payment processing, while advanced LLMs are increasingly capable of managing the negotiation and customer service aspects of debt collection.
tier_rationale: An AI agent for debt collection requires deep integration with each client's account systems, CRM, dialer/telephony infrastructure, and compliance configuration for FDCPA and state-specific rules. The core skills (negotiation, scripted communication) are generic, but per-deployment work — connecting to creditor databases, tuning escalation logic, configuring legal compliance guardrails per jurisdiction — is substantial. This matches T2: same agent product, heavy per-customer configuration.

title: Biochemists and biophysicists
exposure_rationale: This occupation is heavily centered on knowledge work, data analysis, and complex modeling, all of which are being revolutionized by AI (e.g., AlphaFold for protein structure prediction). While physical laboratory work and team management provide a buffer, a significant portion of the role involves literature review, grant writing, and data interpretation—tasks where AI can exponentially increase productivity or automate core analytical functions.
tier_rationale: Much of the work involves wet-lab experimentation and tacit scientific judgment — designing experiments, interpreting ambiguous results, and developing research intuition that even expert scientists struggle to articulate. While literature review, data analysis, and writing are genericizable, the core research craft requires the expert themselves to harness-engineer and iterate with an agent. Physical lab work also bottlenecks full automation.

title: Bioengineers and biomedical engineers
exposure_rationale: This occupation involves heavy digital knowledge work, including software design, statistical modeling, and technical writing, all of which are highly susceptible to AI augmentation and automation. While physical tasks like equipment maintenance and clinical collaboration provide a buffer, the core engineering and research functions are increasingly driven by AI-enhanced simulations and data analysis.
tier_rationale: Bioengineering work involves designing physical medical devices, implants, and biological systems where deep tacit expertise (clinical intuition, material behavior, biological response) intersects with hands-on lab/clinical iteration. Much of the value comes from expert judgment that's hard to fully articulate—the engineer's feel for whether a device will perform safely in vivo—and requires close human-in-the-loop harness engineering. While portions of the work (simulation, documentation, literature review) are genericizable, the core design and validation work requires expert-led agent iteration.

title: Bookkeeping, accounting, and auditing clerks
exposure_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital and involves routine information processing, data entry, and mathematical verification—tasks that AI and automated software already perform with high accuracy. The BLS already projects a decline in employment due to technological change, and the shift toward 'analytical and advisory' roles is increasingly being encroached upon by advanced LLMs and specialized financial AI agents.
tier_rationale: An AI agent for bookkeeping needs deep integration into each company's accounting software (QuickBooks, Xero, NetSuite), bank feeds, payroll systems, and chart of accounts, plus configuration to the client's specific categorization rules and reconciliation workflows. The skills are standardized but per-customer setup (connecting ledgers, mapping accounts, tuning approval/escalation logic) is substantial. Products like Pilot and Digits exemplify this T2 shape.

title: Budget analysts
exposure_rationale: Budget analysts perform work that is almost entirely digital, involving data analysis, financial modeling, and report writing—all areas where AI is highly capable. While the role requires human negotiation and communication with stakeholders, the core tasks of consolidating budgets, identifying discrepancies, and performing cost-benefit analyses are highly susceptible to automation and significant productivity gains through AI.
tier_rationale: Budget analysts work with organization-specific financial systems (ERP, government accounting systems), program structures, and approval workflows that require integration and configuration per customer. While the analytical skills are transferable, an agent must be wired into the company's chart of accounts, historical budgets, and reporting formats, plus tuned to its compliance rules and stakeholder communication norms.

title: Cartographers and photogrammetrists
exposure_rationale: The core tasks of collecting, analyzing, and visualizing geospatial data are fundamentally digital and highly susceptible to AI-driven automation. Computer vision and machine learning are already significantly faster than humans at interpreting satellite imagery, LiDAR, and survey data, while generative AI can increasingly automate the design and production of both static and interactive maps.
tier_rationale: Cartography agents would work with GIS/CAD platforms (ArcGIS, QGIS) on customer-specific spatial datasets, projection conventions, and map style guides. While the software is industry-standard, each deployment (local government, engineering firm) requires integration with the customer's spatial databases, data sources (LiDAR feeds, survey systems), and cartographic standards — closer to a configured vertical agent than a horizontal tool.

title: Chemists and materials scientists
exposure_rationale: This occupation is a high-level blend of digital knowledge work and physical laboratory experimentation. AI is already revolutionizing the digital aspects—such as molecular modeling, predictive simulation, and technical reporting—allowing scientists to screen millions of compounds in silico before ever entering a lab. While the physical requirement of conducting experiments and handling chemicals provides a buffer, the massive productivity gains in research and analysis mean AI will fundamentally restructure how these scientists spend their time.
tier_rationale: While computational and analytical portions could be genericized, much of the work depends on physical lab experimentation and tacit experimental intuition (which reactions to try, interpreting anomalous results, hands-on technique) that even experts struggle to articulate. An effective agent would need to be harness-engineered by the scientist themselves, integrated with proprietary lab equipment and iterated closely on bench results. The physical-experimental and tacit-judgment core prevents a clean T2 productization.

title: Claims adjusters, appraisers, examiners, and investigators
exposure_rationale: This occupation is heavily focused on information processing, document review, and data-driven decision-making, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation. While physical inspections and interpersonal negotiations provide some protection, the BLS already projects a decline in employment due to AI's ability to analyze photos of damage and automate claim calculations.
tier_rationale: Claims work requires deep integration with each insurer's policy systems, claims management platforms, fraud detection databases, and company-specific underwriting rules. An agent (like those being built by insurtech vendors) uses similar skills across carriers but must be configured against each customer's policies, escalation workflows, and regulatory compliance setup. Field inspection tasks add some physical component, but the core evaluative/documentary work fits T2.

title: Compensation and benefits managers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is primarily digital and data-driven, involving the analysis of wage trends, benefits structures, and regulatory compliance—tasks where AI excels. While the role requires high-level human judgment for vendor negotiations and strategic decision-making, AI can automate significant portions of the data analysis, reporting, and routine employee communication, leading to higher productivity and potential workforce consolidation.
tier_rationale: An AI agent for this role needs deep integration with the company's HRIS, payroll, benefits vendors, and compensation databases, plus tuning to the organization's specific pay philosophy, job architecture, and regulatory context. The skills are generalizable across companies, but each deployment requires substantial per-customer configuration of data integrations, policy rules, and compliance controls — the classic T2 shape.

title: Compensation, benefits, and job analysis specialists
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally digital and data-driven, involving core tasks like market research, cost analysis, and report generation that align perfectly with AI's strengths. AI can automate the drafting of job descriptions, perform complex compensation benchmarking, and monitor regulatory changes, significantly increasing individual productivity and reducing the need for manual data processing.
tier_rationale: An AI agent for this work would need deep integration with the employer's HRIS (Workday, ADP), compensation survey data subscriptions (Mercer, Radford), benefits carrier systems, and the company's specific job architecture and pay bands. The core skills (analyzing pay data, classifying jobs, ensuring compliance) are transferable across companies, but useful deployment requires per-customer configuration of pay structures, policies, and regulatory contexts. Same product, custom configuration per employer.

title: Compliance officers
exposure_rationale: Compliance work is primarily digital and involves analyzing vast amounts of regulatory text, auditing data, and generating reports—all areas where LLMs and automated systems excel. While high-level judgment and interpersonal investigations provide some insulation, the core tasks of monitoring, risk assessment, and documentation are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and productivity gains.
tier_rationale: Compliance work requires deep integration with company-specific policies, regulatory frameworks for the industry, internal document repositories, audit/training systems, and escalation workflows. While the agent product (regulatory tracking, audit, policy drafting, training generation) is similar across firms, each deployment requires substantial configuration to the customer's regulatory regime, internal controls, and reporting systems. The skills are generic but the agent must be wired into each organization's compliance stack.

title: Computer and information research scientists
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally digital, involving high-level coding, algorithm design, and data analysis—all areas where AI is rapidly advancing. While these scientists are the ones building AI, the tools they create are increasingly capable of automating their own core tasks, such as writing code, simplifying algorithms, and analyzing experimental results, leading to extreme productivity gains and role restructuring.
tier_rationale: Research scientists produce novel ideas, theories, and experimental designs at the frontier of computing — the tacit judgment about what problems matter, which approaches will work, and how to interpret ambiguous results is largely inarticulable and lives with the individual researcher. An agent here is best built by the researcher themselves through harness engineering and tight iteration, augmenting literature review, coding experiments, and analysis. Horizontal agents help with subtasks, but the creative research core resists genericization or extraction.

title: Computer and information systems managers
exposure_rationale: This occupation is predominantly digital and knowledge-based, involving strategic planning, analysis, and technical oversight that AI can significantly augment. While high-level leadership, vendor negotiation, and personnel management provide a buffer of human judgment, the core tasks of assessing IT needs, monitoring security, and directing technical workflows are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation.
tier_rationale: IT manager work centers on relational and organizational judgment: aligning IT strategy with executives, directing staff, negotiating with vendors, and making resource allocation decisions specific to the organization's politics and priorities. An agent could augment portable parts (vendor analysis, project planning, status reports) but the core leadership, stakeholder relationships, and accountability stay with the human manager.

title: Computer hardware engineers
exposure_rationale: The core work of designing schematics, analyzing circuits, and testing components is increasingly performed using digital Electronic Design Automation (EDA) tools that are highly susceptible to AI-driven optimization and automation. While there is a physical component to testing and manufacturing oversight, the vast majority of the engineering lifecycle is digital and benefits significantly from AI's ability to optimize complex systems and generate code/hardware descriptions.
tier_rationale: Hardware engineering involves deep tacit expertise about physical design trade-offs, signal integrity, timing closure, thermal behavior, and manufacturability that experienced engineers often can't fully articulate. While EDA tools are somewhat standardized, the work is tightly coupled to proprietary chip architectures, fab process nodes, and accumulated design intuition that resists extraction. An effective agent would need to be harness-engineered by the expert engineers themselves, iterating on specific design problems rather than deployed as a generic or configured product.

title: Computer network architects
exposure_rationale: The core work of designing, documenting, and configuring networks is fundamentally digital and data-driven, making it highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and optimization tools. While physical hardware deployment and complex stakeholder management provide a slight buffer, AI is rapidly advancing in automated network topology design, security configuration, and predictive troubleshooting, which will significantly increase individual productivity and restructure the role.
tier_rationale: Network architects design networks tightly coupled to a specific organization's existing infrastructure, vendor stack, security posture, and physical/cloud topology. An agent for this work would need integrations into the company's network management systems, configuration databases, and vendor APIs, plus tuning to organizational standards — same product across customers but heavy per-deployment configuration. Skills are transferable but the agent cannot do useful work without substantial customer-specific context wiring.

title: Computer programmers
exposure_rationale: The core tasks of writing, testing, and debugging code are entirely digital and align perfectly with the strengths of Large Language Models, which are already highly proficient in these domains. The BLS specifically projects a decline in this occupation due to automation and AI, as these tools allow fewer workers to produce the same volume of code and shift higher-level logic to software developers.
tier_rationale: Programming work uses standard tools (IDEs, git, compilers) and codebases accessible via the file system. A horizontal coding agent like Claude Code or Cursor can do the work at any company by simply being pointed at the codebase—no per-customer integration or system-prompt tuning required.

title: Computer support specialists
exposure_rationale: The core tasks of diagnosing technical issues, documenting problems, and guiding users through digital workflows are highly susceptible to AI automation via advanced chatbots and LLM-driven troubleshooting. While some physical hardware setup and complex onsite networking remain, the vast majority of the work is digital information processing that AI can increasingly handle, as evidenced by the BLS's projected decline in employment due to automation.
tier_rationale: An AI agent for computer support (think Decagon/Intercom Fin for IT helpdesk) requires deep per-customer integration: ticketing systems (ServiceNow, Jira), the company's specific software stack, network monitoring tools, knowledge bases of internal procedures, and escalation logic tuned to the org. The skills are generic but each deployment needs months of configuration to access the right systems and learn company-specific issues.

title: Computer systems analysts
exposure_rationale: This occupation is fundamentally digital, involving data modeling, system design, and technical analysis that can be performed entirely on a computer. AI is highly capable of automating core tasks such as writing technical documentation, generating system diagrams, analyzing cost-benefit data, and assisting with the programming and debugging aspects of the role.
tier_rationale: Computer systems analysts require deep knowledge of an organization's specific existing systems, business processes, stakeholders, and industry context to design improvements — this tacit organizational knowledge dominates the work and prevents a horizontal agent from being useful. However, this context could be extracted through structured discovery (interviewing stakeholders, analyzing system documentation, mapping data flows), after which the agent would resemble a T2 product with per-company integrations into the org's IT stack. The liaison/translation work between management and IT is documentable rather than genuinely ineffable.

title: Cost estimators
exposure_rationale: Cost estimation is a data-intensive, digital-first occupation centered on analyzing blueprints, technical documents, and historical databases to predict costs. AI and advanced software are already driving a projected decline in employment by automating routine calculations and simulations, though human oversight remains necessary for complex site-specific variables and high-stakes negotiations.
tier_rationale: Cost estimation requires standard skills (reading blueprints, analyzing materials/labor costs) but an AI agent must integrate with company-specific estimation software, historical cost databases, supplier pricing systems, and BIM/CAD tooling. Each company has proprietary cost benchmarks and bidding workflows that require configuration, making this a genericizable framework with custom per-customer deployment.

title: Court reporters and simultaneous captioners
exposure_rationale: The core product of this occupation is a verbatim digital transcript, a task where AI speech-to-text and natural language processing are already highly proficient. While legal requirements for human oversight and the need to clarify inaudible speech provide a temporary buffer, the rapid advancement of AI in transcription and real-time captioning creates extreme pressure for automation and massive productivity gains that reduce the need for human workers.
tier_rationale: Court reporting and captioning is fundamentally speech-to-text transcription with standardized outputs (verbatim transcripts, closed captions) across all customers. An AI agent (ASR + diarization + legal/domain vocabulary) works the same way at any court or broadcast — no per-customer integration is needed beyond access to the audio feed. The work is highly genericizable, which is why ASR technology is already displacing the role.

title: Credit counselors
exposure_rationale: The core tasks of credit counseling—analyzing financial data, creating budgets, and explaining debt management options—are fundamentally digital and information-based, making them highly susceptible to AI automation. While the role requires interpersonal skills and emotional support for clients in financial distress, AI can already perform the complex calculations and personalized recommendation generation that constitute the bulk of the technical work.
tier_rationale: Credit counselors do largely standardized advisory work (assessing finances, building debt management plans, explaining options), but an agent serving this role at scale needs integration with each employer's client management system, creditor negotiation workflows, and compliance/regulatory configurations (especially in housing or student aid contexts). The skills are transferable but the deployment requires per-customer system integration and tuning, fitting the T2 pattern.

title: Customer service representatives
exposure_rationale: The core duties of this role—answering questions, processing orders, and resolving complaints—are fundamentally digital and information-based, making them highly susceptible to AI-driven automation. Large Language Models and AI agents are already capable of handling complex natural language interactions, and the BLS specifically projects a decline in employment due to automation in this sector.
tier_rationale: A CSR agent (e.g., Decagon, Sierra, Ada) is a generic framework but requires per-customer integration into the company's CRM/ticketing systems (Zendesk, Salesforce), knowledge bases, and escalation workflows, plus system-prompt tuning on company-specific products, policies, and tone. The skills are transferable but each enterprise deployment involves substantial configuration and eval work, which is the defining T2 shape.

title: Data scientists
exposure_rationale: Data science is a fully digital occupation centered on coding, statistical modeling, and data analysis—all areas where AI is rapidly achieving parity or superiority. While human judgment is still needed for business context and ethical oversight, AI can now automate significant portions of the data pipeline, including cleaning raw data, generating complex code, and even suggesting optimal model architectures.
tier_rationale: A data science agent needs access to the company's specific data warehouses, schemas, business metrics definitions, and domain context to produce useful analysis — generic horizontal agents can't meaningfully analyze a company's data without integration into their data stack (Snowflake, dbt, BI tools) and configuration around their KPIs. The skills are transferable but each deployment requires substantial per-customer setup of data connections, semantic layers, and stakeholder context. This mirrors the T2 pattern where the agent product is similar across companies but enterprise deployment requires meaningful integration work.

title: Database administrators and architects
exposure_rationale: This occupation is entirely digital, involving coding (SQL), system architecture, and data management—all areas where AI is exceptionally proficient. AI can already automate routine DBA tasks like performance tuning, query optimization, and security monitoring, while LLMs are highly capable of generating and debugging the complex schemas and scripts used by database architects.
tier_rationale: DBA work requires deep integration with a company's specific database systems, schemas, security policies, and operational tooling. While the skills (SQL, performance tuning, backup/restore) are transferable, an AI agent serving a customer needs configured access to their particular DBMS instances, internal data models, and compliance controls — making it a genericizable framework with substantial per-customer configuration.

title: Desktop publishers
exposure_rationale: The work product is entirely digital and involves routine information processing, layout design, and basic editing—all areas where AI is highly proficient. Generative AI and automated layout tools can now handle text integration, image adjustment, and formatting with minimal human intervention, leading to a projected decline in the occupation as these tasks are automated or absorbed by other roles using AI tools.
tier_rationale: Desktop publishing uses standard software (InDesign, QuarkXPress, etc.) with standard inputs (text, graphics) and outputs (print-ready or web files) across all employers. A horizontal AI agent with access to these standard tools and the project files could handle layout work at any company without per-customer integration or configuration. The work is highly genericizable, similar to graphic design or copywriting.

title: Drafters
exposure_rationale: Drafters perform work that is almost entirely digital, converting conceptual sketches into technical CAD and BIM models. AI is rapidly advancing in generative design and automated technical drawing, allowing for the immediate translation of engineering specifications into precise models, which significantly reduces the human labor required for these tasks.
tier_rationale: Drafters convert engineer/architect sketches into technical CAD/BIM drawings using standard industry tools (AutoCAD, Revit, SolidWorks). An AI agent connected to these standard CAD file formats can perform the work across firms without per-customer integration — the project files themselves provide the context. Specialization by domain (mechanical/architectural/electrical) is captured in the agent's training, not in customer-specific configuration.

title: Economists
exposure_rationale: Economists perform work that is almost entirely digital, involving data analysis, mathematical modeling, and technical writing. AI is exceptionally proficient at the core tasks of this role, including processing large datasets, identifying statistical patterns, and generating reports, which significantly increases productivity and reduces the number of human workers needed for routine analysis.
tier_rationale: Economist work — research, statistical analysis, modeling, forecasting, and writing reports — uses standard tools (Stata, R, Python, Excel) and produces standard outputs (papers, forecasts, policy memos). A horizontal agent with access to the economist's data files and standard analysis tools can do the work without per-employer integration. While some government roles have specific data systems, the core analytical work is genericizable across employers.

title: Editors
exposure_rationale: Editing is a fully digital occupation centered on language processing, a core strength of Large Language Models. AI can already perform high-level copy editing, fact-checking, and rewriting, significantly increasing individual productivity and reducing the total number of human editors required for routine content management.
tier_rationale: Editing work uses standard tools (Word, Google Docs, CMS platforms) and standard inputs/outputs (manuscripts in, edited text out) across publishers. A horizontal AI agent given access to the manuscript files can perform copyediting, fact-checking, and revision suggestions without per-customer integration. Style-guide adherence is just a prompt/reference document, not a deep enterprise integration.

title: Electrical and electronics engineers
exposure_rationale: The core work of designing circuits, modeling systems, and writing documentation is digital and highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and optimization. While physical testing, site visits, and manufacturing supervision provide a buffer, the rapid advancement of AI in CAD, simulation, and coding significantly increases the productivity and exposure of the knowledge-work components.
tier_rationale: Electrical/electronics engineering involves deep tacit judgment about physical systems—knowing whether a circuit will have signal integrity issues, EMI problems, thermal failures, or manufacturability concerns. While CAD tools are standard, the engineering intuition for tradeoffs in real hardware often can't be fully articulated and requires close iteration with the expert. Unlike software where the artifact is fully digital and testable, hardware involves physical site visits, supervising manufacturing, and embodied judgment that resists full extraction.

title: Epidemiologists
exposure_rationale: Epidemiology is a data-intensive knowledge profession where core tasks like statistical analysis, pattern recognition in large datasets, and literature reviews are highly susceptible to AI augmentation. While the role requires human judgment for policy-making, community outreach, and field investigations, the shift toward digital health records and automated surveillance systems significantly increases the productivity and exposure of the occupation.
tier_rationale: An epidemiologist agent needs to integrate with specific public health data systems (state/local surveillance databases, EHRs, CDC reporting pipelines) and be configured to a jurisdiction's reporting requirements, disease priorities, and stakeholder communication channels. The analytical skills (biostatistics, study design, report writing) are generic across employers, but useful deployment requires per-customer data integrations and workflow tuning. This matches T2 rather than T1 (not pure horizontal tool use) or T3 (knowledge is largely codified in epidemiological methods).

title: Fashion designers
exposure_rationale: Fashion design is increasingly digital, utilizing CAD and graphics software for sketching, trend analysis, and virtual modeling—all areas where generative AI is rapidly advancing. While the physical aspects of selecting fabrics, creating prototypes, and overseeing production provide a buffer, the core creative and analytical tasks are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and productivity gains.
tier_rationale: Fashion design uses standard creative tools (CAD programs like CLO 3D, Adobe Illustrator, graphics editing software) that are common across the industry. An AI agent could assist with trend research, sketching, fabric selection, and design iteration by connecting to these standard tools without per-enterprise integration. The work output (designs, prototypes, tech packs) is portable across employers.

title: Financial analysts
exposure_rationale: Financial analysts perform work that is almost entirely digital, involving the processing of large datasets, trend analysis, and the generation of reports—all areas where AI excels. While high-level strategy and client relationships provide some insulation, the core tasks of data evaluation, risk assessment, and financial modeling are being rapidly automated or significantly enhanced by AI, leading to major restructuring of the occupation.
tier_rationale: An AI agent for financial analysts needs to ingest enterprise-specific data (internal financial models, proprietary research databases, Bloomberg/FactSet/Capital IQ feeds, portfolio management systems) and be tuned to the firm's investment thesis, risk frameworks, and reporting templates. While the analytical skills are generalizable, deployment requires substantial per-firm integration with data providers and internal systems, plus configuration to firm-specific methodologies. This is the classic T2 shape: same agent product, months of integration per enterprise.

title: Financial clerks
exposure_rationale: Financial clerks perform routine, digital-first tasks such as data entry, record updating, and basic financial calculations that are highly susceptible to AI automation. While some roles involve customer interaction, the core functions—processing claims, verifying credit, and managing payroll—are increasingly handled by AI-driven software, leading to a projected decline in employment.
tier_rationale: Financial clerks do transactional record-keeping work tied to enterprise-specific systems (billing platforms, payroll systems, EHRs, insurance policy databases, brokerage back-office systems). An AI agent for this work requires per-enterprise integrations into those systems plus configuration of the company's specific workflows, fee schedules, and approval rules. The skills are generic but deployment demands substantial customization per customer.

title: Financial examiners
exposure_rationale: Financial examiners perform work that is almost entirely digital, involving the analysis of balance sheets, loan documentation, and regulatory text. AI is exceptionally well-suited for automating the core tasks of this role, such as identifying anomalies in financial data, summarizing meeting minutes, and checking compliance against complex legal frameworks, though human judgment remains necessary for final risk assessments and high-level management evaluations.
tier_rationale: Financial examiners review balance sheets, loan documents, and compliance with regulations at specific institutions. An AI agent would need integration into each institution's core banking systems, loan management platforms, and document repositories, plus tuning to the specific regulatory frameworks (federal vs state, risk vs consumer compliance) the examiner operates under. The skills generalize across employers but deployment requires substantial per-institution integration and configuration.

title: Financial managers
exposure_rationale: Financial management is a predominantly digital knowledge-work occupation focused on data analysis, reporting, and forecasting, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While the role requires significant human judgment, strategic advisory, and leadership, the core technical tasks of monitoring financial details and preparing statements are being rapidly transformed by AI tools.
tier_rationale: Financial managers analyze data, prepare reports, and advise executives — work that requires deep integration with enterprise-specific ERP/accounting systems (SAP, Oracle, NetSuite), internal financial databases, and industry/regulatory context (healthcare billing, government appropriations). An AI agent would need per-enterprise integrations and configuration to ledgers, chart of accounts, and reporting workflows, plus tuning to the org's policies and compliance rules. There is also a relational/advisory component with executives, but the core analytical work is a genericizable framework with heavy custom configuration.

title: Fundraisers
exposure_rationale: Fundraising involves significant digital work including donor research, data analysis, and the creation of promotional messaging, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While major gift solicitation and event management require high-level interpersonal skills and physical presence, the majority of the administrative, analytical, and communication-based tasks are moving toward AI-driven workflows.
tier_rationale: Fundraising centers on cultivating and maintaining personal relationships with donors—especially major gifts and planned giving—where trust, rapport, and in-person engagement are the core value. An AI agent can handle research, prospect identification, drafting appeals, and campaign analytics, but the relational core of soliciting and stewarding donors stays with the human.

title: General office clerks
exposure_rationale: The core duties of this role—data entry, document formatting, scheduling, and information processing—are almost entirely digital and routine, making them highly susceptible to AI automation. While some physical tasks like handling mail or greeting visitors remain, the vast majority of the workload can be absorbed by AI agents and automated software, as reflected in the BLS's projected decline for the occupation.
tier_rationale: An agent for general office clerks would handle phone routing, scheduling, document filing/retrieval, data entry, and bill processing — tasks that look similar across companies but depend on each employer's specific systems (phone systems, CRM, document repositories, EHRs for hospitals, SIS for colleges). A horizontal agent can't just plug in; deployment requires integrating with the enterprise's office stack and tuning workflows to local procedures. The skills are generic but the per-enterprise integration burden makes it T2.

title: Geographers
exposure_rationale: Geographers primarily perform digital knowledge work, including data analysis, GIS modeling, and report writing, all of which are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While some fieldwork exists, the core of the profession involves processing satellite imagery and census data—tasks where AI and machine learning are rapidly becoming more efficient than humans.
tier_rationale: Geographers' work centers on GIS analysis, remote sensing, and spatial data interpretation using standard tools (ArcGIS, QGIS), but deployment at a government agency or firm requires integration with that organization's proprietary geospatial databases, satellite/imagery feeds, and domain-specific data layers (population, infrastructure, environmental). An agent product would generalize across geographers but need per-enterprise data pipeline configuration and workflow tuning. This puts it in T2 rather than T1.

title: Graphic designers
exposure_rationale: Graphic design is a fundamentally digital occupation where core tasks—image generation, layout creation, and photo editing—are being rapidly transformed by generative AI. While high-level creative direction and client management remain human-centric, the automation of production tasks significantly increases individual productivity, likely reducing the total number of designers needed for routine commercial work.
tier_rationale: Graphic design uses standard professional tools (Adobe Creative Suite, Figma) that are used across every company. A horizontal AI agent given access to brand assets and project files could produce designs at any enterprise without per-customer integration work. The inputs (briefs, references) and outputs (visual files) are standardized across the industry.

title: Health information technologists and medical registrars
exposure_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, involving the analysis, organization, and validation of clinical data within computerized systems. AI is exceptionally well-suited for core tasks like data abstraction, classification coding, and generating reports from large datasets, which significantly increases the risk of automation and restructuring.
tier_rationale: The work requires deep integration with enterprise-specific EHR systems (Epic, Cerner), clinical databases, and disease registries, plus tuning to institution-specific coding practices, data schemas, and compliance requirements (HIPAA). An agent product could serve many healthcare organizations but each deployment requires substantial integration into the hospital's specific information systems and validation workflows, mirroring the T2 pattern of medical scribing tools like Abridge.

title: High school teachers
exposure_rationale: High school teachers are heavily exposed because core digital tasks like lesson planning, content creation, and grading are highly susceptible to AI automation and augmentation. While the physical requirement of classroom management and interpersonal mentorship provides a buffer, AI's ability to provide personalized tutoring and automated feedback significantly reshapes the instructional delivery and administrative workload of the profession.
tier_rationale: Teaching is fundamentally relational — value comes from sustained relationships with specific students, classroom management, motivation, and in-person supervision. An AI agent can augment portable parts (lesson planning, grading, generating materials) but the core instructional and relational work stays with the human teacher in a physical classroom.

title: Historians
exposure_rationale: Historians perform high-level knowledge work—researching, analyzing, and writing—that is increasingly digital and highly susceptible to AI-driven efficiency gains in document processing and synthesis. While the job requires human judgment and physical interaction with artifacts or archives, the core output of reports and articles faces significant restructuring as AI tools become capable of parsing vast historical datasets and drafting complex narratives.
tier_rationale: Historian work involves research, analysis, and writing using standard sources (archives, documents, books) and standard authoring tools. A horizontal AI agent with research, document analysis, and writing capabilities can perform this work across employers by being pointed at the relevant archives/sources, without per-enterprise integration or system-prompt tuning.

title: Human resources managers
exposure_rationale: Human resources management is a digital-heavy knowledge occupation where AI can significantly automate recruitment screening, payroll processing, and regulatory compliance reporting. While the role requires high-level interpersonal skills for dispute mediation and strategic leadership, the vast majority of the administrative and analytical tasks are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and restructuring.
tier_rationale: HR managers' work centers on relationships with executives, employees, and union counterparts—mediating disputes, advising leaders, handling grievances, and serving as the trusted link between management and staff. While an agent could automate portable pieces (policy drafting, recruiting workflows, payroll oversight, compliance checks), the core consultative and mediation work depends on established trust and standing within the organization. Deployment would also require enterprise-specific integrations (HRIS, payroll), but the binding constraint is relational.

title: Human resources specialists
exposure_rationale: The core tasks of recruiting, screening, and administrative record-keeping are highly digital and data-driven, making them prime targets for AI automation and augmentation. While the role requires significant interpersonal interaction and human judgment for interviewing and employee relations, AI can now handle complex scheduling, initial candidate vetting, and policy inquiries, significantly increasing individual worker productivity and reducing the need for entry-level administrative staff.
tier_rationale: An HR agent needs deep integration with enterprise-specific systems (ATS like Workday/Greenhouse, HRIS, payroll, benefits providers) and tuning to each organization's policies, compliance requirements, and workflows. The core skills (screening, interviewing, policy guidance) are similar across employers, but deployment requires per-enterprise configuration of integrations, policy knowledge bases, and escalation logic. This matches the T2 pattern of a generic agent product with substantial customization per customer.

title: Industrial designers
exposure_rationale: Industrial design is a predominantly digital occupation where core tasks like sketching, 3D modeling, and rendering are being rapidly transformed by generative AI and advanced CAD automation. While the role requires physical prototyping and cross-functional collaboration, the vast majority of the design process is computer-based, making it highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and workflow restructuring.
tier_rationale: Industrial designers work primarily in standard professional tools (CAD/CAID software like SolidWorks, Rhino, Figma for UI) that an AI agent can interface with the same way at any firm. The work product (sketches, 3D models, prototypes, specs) is portable across employers and doesn't require per-enterprise system integration. While some tacit aesthetic judgment exists, the core design workflow is genericizable like other creative/engineering tool-based roles.

title: Industrial engineers
exposure_rationale: Industrial engineering is heavily focused on data analysis, systems design, and process optimization—tasks where AI and machine learning excel. While the role requires physical site visits and human collaboration to understand workflows, the core work product is digital analysis and modeling, making the occupation highly susceptible to significant productivity gains and restructuring through AI-driven automation.
tier_rationale: Industrial engineering work depends heavily on tacit knowledge of specific factory layouts, production processes, worker behaviors, and physical observation of operations — none of which a horizontal agent can access. However, this context is documentable through process maps, time studies, sensor data, and structured interviews, which once captured could enable a per-enterprise agent (T2-like) to analyze data and recommend optimizations. The physical observation and cross-functional collaboration aspects make it harder than pure software roles, placing it in T3a.

title: Information clerks
exposure_rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because its core tasks—processing routine data, answering inquiries, and maintaining records—are fundamentally digital and information-based. While some roles like hotel desk clerks or airport agents require a physical presence, the majority of the work involves routine information processing that is increasingly being automated by AI-driven self-service systems, chatbots, and automated data entry tools.
tier_rationale: Information clerks do routine clerical work that's similar across employers in skill terms, but automating it requires deep integration with each enterprise's specific systems — hotel PMS, court docketing systems, government eligibility databases, HRIS, order-entry platforms. A clerk-automation agent product can serve many enterprises, but each deployment requires connecting to bespoke records systems and tuning workflows to local policies and escalation rules.

title: Information security analysts
exposure_rationale: This occupation is entirely digital, involving tasks like monitoring networks, analyzing vulnerabilities, and writing reports that are highly susceptible to AI automation. While AI increases the demand for security to counter AI-driven threats, it also significantly automates the core functions of threat detection, log analysis, and incident response, drastically increasing individual worker productivity.
tier_rationale: An infosec agent needs deep integration with the enterprise's specific security stack (SIEM, firewalls, EDR, IAM, ticketing) and tuning to the organization's network topology, asset inventory, and risk posture. While the skills and frameworks are standardized across companies, deploying an agent requires substantial per-enterprise configuration of detection rules, escalation paths, and compliance controls. Same product, many enterprise integrations.

title: Instructional coordinators
exposure_rationale: Instructional coordinators perform high-level knowledge work including curriculum development, data analysis, and educational material review, all of which are digital-first tasks highly susceptible to AI augmentation. While the role requires significant interpersonal interaction for teacher coaching and stakeholder management, the core 'product'—curricula and instructional strategies—can be generated and optimized much faster using generative AI and advanced analytics.
tier_rationale: Instructional coordinators' work centers on relationships with teachers, principals, and administrators—mentoring, coaching, observing classrooms, and negotiating curriculum changes through trust built over time. While an agent could augment portable parts (analyzing test data, drafting curriculum materials, summarizing research on teaching techniques), the core relational work of persuading and developing teachers stays with the human. Per-district context also matters but the binding constraint is interpersonal.

title: Insurance sales agents
exposure_rationale: The core tasks of analyzing policies, customizing insurance programs, and maintaining records are digital and data-driven, making them highly susceptible to AI automation and augmentation. While the role requires interpersonal trust and persuasive sales skills, AI-powered chatbots and recommendation engines are increasingly capable of handling routine inquiries and policy comparisons, significantly increasing individual agent productivity and reducing the need for human intervention in simple transactions.
tier_rationale: Insurance sales is fundamentally relational — agents build trust with clients, generate referrals, and maintain ongoing relationships for renewals and claims support. An AI agent could automate quoting, policy comparison, application processing, and follow-ups (T2-style with carrier system integrations), but the core client acquisition and trust-building stays with the human. The relational core dominates the value proposition, especially for independent agents who compete on advice and rapport.

title: Insurance underwriters
exposure_rationale: Underwriting is a fundamentally digital occupation centered on routine information processing, risk modeling, and data analysis—all areas where AI excels. The BLS already projects a decline in employment due to automation, and the shift from traditional software to advanced AI will likely automate even complex, non-standard risk assessments that previously required human judgment.
tier_rationale: Underwriting agents require deep integration with each carrier's policy administration systems, rating engines, proprietary risk models, and underwriting guidelines that vary by line of business and state regulations. While the core skills (risk analysis, applying guidelines) are similar across insurers, deployment requires per-enterprise configuration of decision rules, escalation logic, and connections to internal data sources and external feeds (MIB, credit bureaus, MVR).

title: Interior designers
exposure_rationale: Interior design is heavily reliant on digital tools like CAD and BIM, which are being rapidly transformed by generative AI capable of creating photorealistic renderings and floor plans from text prompts. While the job requires physical site visits, material inspections, and complex interpersonal coordination with contractors, the core creative and technical drafting tasks are highly susceptible to AI-driven automation and productivity gains.
tier_rationale: Interior design is heavily relational — designers spend significant time soliciting clients, understanding their personal tastes and lifestyles, and coordinating with contractors and architects on-site. While an agent could help with CAD work, material specification, and renderings (portable tasks), the client relationship, on-site visits, and trust-based collaboration with builders remain with the human. Aesthetic judgment also has a tacit component, but the dominant constraint is the client-relationship core of the business.

title: Interpreters and translators
exposure_rationale: The core work product is purely digital information (text and speech) in a domain where Large Language Models already demonstrate near-human proficiency. While high-stakes interpreting (legal, medical) and sign language still require human presence and nuanced judgment, the vast majority of translation and routine interpreting tasks are being rapidly automated or shifted to 'post-editing' roles, significantly reducing the total human labor required per unit of work.
tier_rationale: Translation has standard inputs (source text) and outputs (target text), with universal CAT tools and well-established workflows across industries. A horizontal AI agent (LLM-based translation) can perform this work without per-enterprise integration — just give it the documents. Live interpreting has performative/physical elements but the core knowledge work is genericizable.

title: Judges and hearing officers
exposure_rationale: The core tasks of researching legal issues, evaluating documents, and writing opinions are highly digital and susceptible to AI automation, which can significantly increase productivity and reduce the need for human staff. However, the role requires high-stakes human judgment, presiding over physical courtrooms, and maintaining public trust, which creates a significant barrier to full automation.
tier_rationale: Judicial decision-making relies on courtroom demeanor assessment, weighing credibility, judicial temperament, and discretion that even judges struggle to fully articulate. Beyond tacit gestalt, the role carries constitutional/societal authority that society won't delegate to an agent — though if one tried, only the judge could harness-engineer a tool to assist their own reasoning. Research, drafting opinions, and reviewing briefs can be augmented, but the core adjudicative judgment is genuinely tacit.

title: Labor relations specialists
exposure_rationale: This occupation is predominantly digital and knowledge-based, involving the drafting of contracts, policy development, and legal compliance—all areas where AI excels. While the high-stakes interpersonal negotiation and dispute resolution provide a buffer, AI will significantly automate the research, document generation, and investigative analysis components of the role.
tier_rationale: The core of labor relations work is negotiating contracts, resolving grievances, and building trust between management, unions, and workers — value lives in established relationships and credibility with specific counterparties at the bargaining table. An agent could help draft contracts, analyze labor law, and prepare materials, but the relational negotiation and dispute resolution core stays with the human. Secondary tacit elements (reading the room in tense negotiations) also resist full extraction.

title: Landscape architects
exposure_rationale: Landscape architecture is a predominantly digital knowledge-based occupation where core tasks like CADD modeling, site analysis, and cost estimation are highly susceptible to AI automation and enhancement. While the role requires physical site visits and interpersonal client management, the generative design capabilities of AI can significantly accelerate the creative and technical drafting phases, potentially reducing the total human hours required per project.
tier_rationale: Landscape architects use standard professional tools (CADD, GIS) to produce site plans, models, and specifications — inputs and outputs are largely standardized across firms. A horizontal agent connected to these standard design tools and project files could perform the design work without per-enterprise integration. Site-specific data (surveys, environmental reports) is project-level context rather than enterprise-level customization.

title: Lawyers
exposure_rationale: Lawyers perform high-level knowledge work that is almost entirely digital, involving document drafting, legal research, and complex data analysis—all areas where LLMs excel. While courtroom advocacy and high-stakes interpersonal negotiation provide a protective barrier, the vast majority of billable hours are spent on tasks that AI can now significantly accelerate or automate, leading to major structural changes in the profession.
tier_rationale: Lawyers' work requires deep integration with firm-specific document management systems, matter databases, precedent libraries, and client files, plus tuning to jurisdiction and practice-area workflows—Harvey-style deployments require months of per-firm configuration. While the core skills (research, drafting, analysis) are similar across firms, the agent product needs custom integrations and workflow tuning per enterprise. Senior partner work with key client relationships would shade toward T3c, but the bulk of lawyer work (associates doing research, drafting, document review) fits T2.

title: Librarians and library media specialists
exposure_rationale: Librarianship is fundamentally about information retrieval, organization, and research—domains where LLMs and AI search engines excel. While the physical presence in schools and public libraries provides a buffer through community programming and interpersonal support, the core technical tasks of cataloging, database management, and reference assistance are highly exposed to AI automation and displacement.
tier_rationale: Librarian work involves significant physical/in-person components (story time, circulation desk, patron interaction) but the knowledge core—cataloging, reference research, collection management—requires integration with each library's specific ILS (Integrated Library System like Alma, Sierra, Koha), local catalog, patron database, and consortium resources. An agent could provide reference and cataloging assistance generically, but enterprise deployment requires per-institution integration with collection systems and tuning to local collection policies and patron populations.

title: Loan officers
exposure_rationale: The core tasks of loan officers—analyzing financial data, verifying documentation, and assessing risk—are fundamentally digital and highly susceptible to AI automation. While complex commercial loans and relationship-based mortgage sales still require human judgment and interpersonal skills, the rapid advancement of automated underwriting and AI-driven customer service significantly reduces the need for human intervention in routine loan processing.
tier_rationale: Loan officer work requires deep integration with each lender's loan origination system (LOS), underwriting software, credit bureau feeds, compliance/regulatory rules, and product-specific terms — all of which vary by institution. An AI agent would be a horizontal product (analyze applications, verify documents, run underwriting logic, communicate with applicants) but every deployment requires connecting to the bank's core systems and tuning to its credit policies and state/federal compliance workflows. The relational/sales aspects (especially in mortgage and commercial) add some T3c flavor, but the bulk of the role — underwriting and processing — fits T2.

title: Logisticians
exposure_rationale: Logisticians perform high-level knowledge work that is primarily digital, involving data analysis, forecasting, and supply chain optimization—tasks where AI excels. While the role requires human-to-human negotiation and real-world problem-solving for physical disruptions, the core functions of inventory management and route optimization are increasingly being automated or enhanced by sophisticated AI algorithms.
tier_rationale: A logistician agent requires deep integration with enterprise-specific ERP, WMS, TMS, and procurement systems (SAP, Oracle, Manhattan), plus tuning to each company's supplier network, SKUs, routing rules, and escalation workflows. The skills are generic but every deployment demands months of integration and configuration to the company's supply chain data and processes. This matches T2: same agent product, custom per-enterprise configuration.

title: Management analysts
exposure_rationale: Management analysts perform high-level knowledge work—including data analysis, report writing, and financial modeling—that is highly susceptible to AI augmentation and automation. While the role requires significant interpersonal skills for client interviews and stakeholder management, the core 'back-office' tasks of synthesizing information and developing efficiency recommendations are increasingly being streamlined by AI tools.
tier_rationale: Management consulting blends tacit diagnostic judgment with deep client relationships — proposals are won, access to personnel is granted, and recommendations are accepted largely on the basis of trust with specific executives. An AI agent can do significant portions of the analytical work (data analysis, benchmarking, deck drafting) but the relational core of winning engagements, interviewing personnel, and persuading managers to adopt changes stays with the human. The portable analytic pieces resemble T1 work, but the role's value capture is fundamentally relational.

title: Marine engineers and naval architects
exposure_rationale: The core of this occupation involves digital design, complex mathematical modeling, and technical report writing, all of which are highly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation. While physical site visits to shipyards and offshore platforms provide a buffer, the shift toward AI-integrated CAD, automated performance testing, and generative design for hulls and machinery significantly reshapes the workflow.
tier_rationale: Marine engineering and naval architecture involves deep tacit judgment about hull behavior, structural integrity, and propulsion tradeoffs that even experts struggle to fully articulate, combined with safety-critical physical intuition refined over decades. While CAD and simulation tools are standard, the design judgment for one-off vessels and offshore structures requires close expert iteration rather than a horizontal or configurable agent. An agent for this work would need to be harness-engineered by the senior engineers themselves alongside specialized simulation tools.

title: Market research analysts
exposure_rationale: The core duties of this role—data collection, statistical analysis, trend forecasting, and report generation—are fundamentally digital and align perfectly with AI's strengths in processing large datasets and generating insights. While human judgment is still needed for high-level strategy and client relationships, AI can now automate the vast majority of the technical and administrative tasks that define the daily work of an analyst.
tier_rationale: An AI agent for market research analysts needs access to enterprise-specific data sources (CRM, sales data, customer databases, survey platforms like Qualtrics, social listening tools) and tuning to the company's products, competitive landscape, and reporting conventions. While the analytical skills are generic, deployment requires per-enterprise integration with proprietary data and workflows, plus eval dashboards trusted by internal stakeholders. This matches the T2 pattern of a generic framework with substantial per-customer configuration.

title: Material recording clerks
exposure_rationale: This occupation is highly exposed because its core functions—tracking data, scheduling, and inventory reporting—are digital information-processing tasks that AI and automated systems excel at. While some physical presence is required to inspect goods or handle packages, the rapid integration of AI-driven logistics software, RFID technology, and computer vision for quality control is already leading to a projected decline in employment.
tier_rationale: The work involves tracking inventory and shipments in enterprise systems (WMS, ERP, RFID scanners) that vary by employer. An agent automating this requires per-enterprise integrations into the specific inventory/ERP stack and workflow tuning, similar to other back-office automation. Also includes significant physical warehouse handling, but the recording/scheduling core is T2 knowledge work.

title: Materials engineers
exposure_rationale: Materials engineering is heavily reliant on digital simulation, data analysis, and computational modeling, areas where AI and machine learning are already accelerating material discovery and property prediction. While the role requires physical lab work and manufacturing oversight, a significant portion of the core value—designing alloys, analyzing failures, and writing technical reports—is increasingly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation.
tier_rationale: Materials engineering work depends heavily on proprietary processes, lab equipment, physical testing, and tacit expertise about how materials behave in specific manufacturing contexts. Much of the judgment (failure analysis, material selection trade-offs) is built from hands-on experience with specific products and is hard to fully articulate; an agent would need close iteration with the expert and integration with physical experimentation. Relational and physical lab elements further prevent a generic or merely-configured agent from doing the work.

title: Mathematicians and statisticians
exposure_rationale: This occupation is almost entirely digital, involving data analysis, mathematical modeling, and coding—all domains where AI and Large Language Models excel. While high-level theoretical research and complex interdisciplinary communication require human judgment, AI can now automate significant portions of data cleaning, statistical testing, and code generation, drastically increasing individual productivity and restructuring the workflow.
tier_rationale: An AI agent for this work uses standard statistical/mathematical tooling (R, Python, SAS) but needs deep integration with each enterprise's specific data sources, schemas, and domain context (clinical trial protocols, government survey designs, manufacturing QA pipelines) to deliver useful analyses. The analytical skills are portable, but the value comes from being wired into the organization's data infrastructure and tuned to its reporting conventions and regulatory requirements. This matches the T2 pattern of a genericizable agent framework requiring substantial per-enterprise configuration.

title: Mechanical engineers
exposure_rationale: Mechanical engineering is heavily reliant on digital tools like CAD, simulations, and data analysis, all of which are being rapidly enhanced by AI to automate complex design iterations and predictive modeling. While the role requires physical prototyping, site visits, and hardware testing, the core intellectual work of designing and analyzing mechanical systems is increasingly susceptible to AI-driven productivity gains and automation.
tier_rationale: Mechanical engineering work depends on physical intuition, manufacturability judgment, and integration with hardware/prototyping that involves genuinely tacit expertise — the senior engineer's sense for whether a design will fail under load or be manufacturable. While CAD tools are standardized, the design judgment, multi-physics tradeoffs, and physical testing loops require expert-driven harness engineering rather than a generic or configurable agent. Some sub-tasks (drafting, simulation setup) lean T1, but the core role is dominated by tacit physical-world judgment.

title: Medical dosimetrists
exposure_rationale: The core of this occupation involves complex mathematical calculations, image analysis (CT/MRI), and digital treatment planning, all of which are domains where AI and machine learning excel. While there is a minor physical component involving patient positioning and equipment calibration, the vast majority of the work is digital information processing that is highly susceptible to automation and AI-driven optimization.
tier_rationale: Medical dosimetry is knowledge work centered on radiation dose calculation and treatment planning using specialized software (Eclipse, Pinnacle, RayStation) integrated with hospital imaging systems (CT/MRI), EHRs, and linear accelerator hardware. An agent would need deep per-enterprise integration with each cancer center's planning systems, equipment calibration data, and physician workflows, plus regulatory/QA compliance — similar to how Abridge integrates per-hospital.
